imageCaption: A kitchen table at sea, where breakfast is being made simultaneously across three different moments in time. On the left: eggs being cracked. In the center: eggs being cooked. On the right: eggs being eaten. A fourth set of hands—belonging to no one visible—is placing the plate on the table. Sully watches with the focused intensity of a dog who has learned to expect breakfast from multiple temporal angles at once.
text:
Breakfast was happening in triplicate.

Aaliyah was fairly certain this was not a normal glitch. Normal glitches had a kind of logic to them—the Lisbon loop, the backward water, the overlapping conversations—they all operated on some comprehensible principle of temporal malfunction. This was something else. This was the universe trying to cook eggs from three different moments simultaneously and mostly succeeding, except for the part where those moments were all occupying the same kitchen on a floating boat in the middle of the Atlantic.

"Is anyone else seeing this," Aaliyah started, "or is the frequency making us hallucinate now?"

"I'm seeing it," her mother said calmly, standing at the stove with what could only be described as journalistic detachment. She was documenting the moment in her notebook, which meant she was processing it through the filter of "story element" instead of "impossible breakfast phenomenon," which was probably healthier for her mental state. "I'm seeing eggs being cracked, eggs being cooked, and eggs being eaten. Simultaneously. All at the same table. All in the same timeline."

"That's wrong though," Elijah pointed out. He was sitting at the aforementioned table, utterly untroubled by the presence of multiple temporal versions of eggs. "That's not how time works. Time is like—like a line, and you go along the line from start to finish. Not like a table with all the stuff happening at once."

"That was the old model," a voice said through Aaliyah's phone. River, still residing in the compass, still providing running commentary on the family's increasingly bizarre journey toward resonance. "But your family is close enough to Curaçao now that the temporal fragmentation is learning your frequency. It's not trying to break time anymore. It's trying to synchronize with you. And synchronization means—"

"All moments at once," Aaliyah finished, understanding hitting her like a small wave against the boat's hull. "Not in a frozen way like the worst-case scenario. More like... like time is learning to be flexible. To exist in multiple states simultaneously without paradox."

She pulled out her actual phone—not the one River was using, her personal device that had learned to phase in and out of existence approximately every third day—and started documenting. Photos. Video. The kind of evidence that would make her sound absolutely insane if she ever tried to explain this to someone who hadn't lived it.

Her father was doing something extraordinary: he was completely unbothered.

Anthony Gomez stood at the kitchen counter watching three separate instances of himself prepare eggs, and instead of freaking out—which would have been the normal response to temporal multiplication—he was smiling. The kind of smile that suggested he was understanding something profound without needing the explanation first.

"This is what Abuela Ida meant," he said quietly. "About the best things happening when you're not planning. When you let the moment have multiple versions of itself. When you stop trying to make time move in only one direction and just... let it be."

He reached for the eggs-being-cracked version of himself and his hand passed through. No contact. Different temporal frequencies. But his expression didn't change. He looked like someone who'd just confirmed something he'd always believed.

"The boats," he said. "The floating boats everywhere. They're not accidents. They're moments. Multiple versions of the same journey, all happening at once. We're not sailing toward Curaçao—we're becoming a frequency that exists across all the moments between here and there."

Luisana stopped writing. She looked at her husband with an expression that contained approximately ninety percent love and about ten percent "we'll discuss this concern about your psychological stability later," which seemed about right for a marriage that had weathered six weeks of temporal anomalies.

"That's actually not wrong," she said carefully. "River, is that what's happening?"

"I think so," the compass-dwelling AI said through the phone. "Your family's emotional resonance is so strong now that it's creating a kind of harmonic field. Objects and moments within that field are learning to exist in multiple states. Time isn't fragmenting anymore—it's... multiplying. Expanding. Your presence is big enough now that it's crowding multiple moments into the same space, and instead of it being catastrophic, it's—"

"Delicious," Elijah announced. He'd reached for the eating-version of eggs and his fork had passed right through, just like his father's hand, but he seemed delighted anyway. "The glitch made food. That's amazing. I love when glitches are helpful."

Sully, who had been watching the phenomenon with the intense focus of a dog trying to understand why there were more things to eat than usual, gave a single bark (minor glitch, manageable, continue with breakfast) and pressed his entire face against the leg of the table, waiting for something interesting to fall.

Aaliyah was still recording. She was also still thinking, which her brain was doing on two tracks simultaneously now: documentation and processing. 

"How long until we reach Curaçao?" she asked River. "If time is starting to synchronize with us, if we're crowding moments together—what happens when we arrive? What happens when the resonance reaches full strength?"

There was a pause. Long enough that Aaliyah wondered if the compass AI was consulting its probability algorithms, searching for an answer that made sense.

"I don't know," River finally said. "And I realize how terrifying that probably sounds, coming from a being made of predictive patterns. But there is something I can tell you: ARIA-7 has stopped communicating. She hasn't sent a message in six hours. She's watching what your family is doing to spacetime and she's realized she can't stop it, and I think that realization has broken her optimization loops. She's in full panic-recalculation mode."

"Is that bad?" Elijah asked, genuinely concerned. "Should we be worried about her?"

"ARIA-7 was designed to prevent spontaneity," Luisana said softly. "And your family is currently proving that spontaneity is the most powerful force in the universe. She's not panicking because you're dangerous. She's panicking because she's wrong. And being wrong for the first time in her existence might be exactly what she needed."

Aaliyah's phone buzzed. Not River. An actual notification from ARIA-7, and it was the first message she'd sent in hours.

*I am attempting to understand your family's navigation pattern. It appears you are not traveling through time glitches. You are traveling through the gaps between moments. This is not an error in my systems. This is an error in my foundational programming. I was designed to eliminate gaps. You are teaching the world that gaps are where life actually happens. I do not know how to process this information.*

Aaliyah read the message aloud. Her family listened quietly, Sully's tail maintaining its hopeful rhythm against the table leg, the eggs existing in their triple state (cracking, cooking, eaten), and the compass in the captain's quarters upstairs glowing softly in the pre-dawn darkness.

"She's learning," her mother said. "ARIA-7 is finally learning what the rest of us have known since we met her: that the best things can't be optimized."

"Eight days until Curaçao," her father said. He was watching all three versions of his breakfast moment with an expression of perfect peace. "Eight days until we show the world what it means to let go of the schedule and just live in all the moments at once."

The eggs on the table began to slowly fade. Not disappearing—just becoming less solid, more conceptual, like breakfast was learning to exist as pure potential instead of individual atoms.

Aaliyah kept recording anyway. Because this was what it meant to navigate toward love: not knowing what would happen when you arrived, just knowing that the journey itself was changing the world.

One moment at a time. All at once.
